summary.difficultyAssessment: Current distribution is D1:38 (21%), D2:98 (54%), D3:44 (24%). This skews D2-heavy vs the GL target of ~30:40:30. D1 is under-represented (21% vs target 30%) and D2 is over-represented (54% vs target 40%). The D3 questions (146-180) are genuinely challenging and well-designed, with multi-step problems, three-part ratios, working backwards, and combined-topic problems that reflect real GL D3 patterns. Several D2 questions are arguably D1 difficulty (simple scale factor with clean numbers).
summary.coverageAssessment: Good coverage of sharing in a ratio, finding missing values, recipe scaling, unitary method, and map scales. However, GL research identifies two significant gaps: (1) SIMPLIFYING RATIOS — GL estimates ~15% of ratio questions test this, but there are ZERO simplifying ratio questions in the bank despite lessons existing for it. (2) RATIO-TO-FRACTION CONVERSION — GL estimates ~10% and calls it 'the single most common error children make', but there are ZERO questions testing this. (3) No inverse proportion questions (minor — GL only ~3%). Three-part ratio questions exist but only at D3 (Q146-180 range), missing D2 coverage GL expects.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: Lessons are generally well-structured with good visual components (BarModel, WorkedExample) and the teach-interact-consolidate flow is solid. However, there is a critical mapping gap: 'master-sharing-ratio' sub-concept has 48 questions mapped to it but NO LESSON EXISTS — all show '[No lesson found]'. This means nearly 27% of all questions have no preparatory teaching. The remaining lessons are effective for their sub-concepts, though the unitary method lesson uses a cost-based example while many mapped questions are distance/speed problems, creating a slight contextual mismatch. A duplicate option exists in the simplifying-ratios-mistake interact screen (options show '8:6' twice).
topPriorities: 1. CREATE LESSON for master-sharing-ratio — 48 questions (27% of total) have no preparatory lesson, including all D3 sharing/working-backwards questions
2. ADD SIMPLIFYING RATIO QUESTIONS — GL estimates 15% coverage, currently 0 questions. Lessons already exist for this sub-concept but nothing to practise
3. ADD RATIO-TO-FRACTION CONVERSION QUESTIONS — GL calls this the #1 misconception children make. 0 coverage currently

issues.title: 48 questions mapped to master-sharing-ratio but NO lesson exists
issues.description: The sub-concept 'master-sharing-ratio' has 48 questions mapped to it (27% of total, including 22 D3 questions), but the mapping shows '[No lesson found]' for all of them. This means nearly a third of all ratio questions — including the most challenging D3 multi-step, three-part ratio, and working-backwards problems — have zero preparatory teaching. A child encountering these questions in Focused Learning mode would get no lesson first.
issues.suggestedFix: Create 2 lessons for master-sharing-ratio: (1) a step-by-step lesson teaching the 'add parts, divide total, multiply each part' method for two-part sharing, and (2) a lesson covering three-part sharing and working backwards from one share. These are the core GL ratio skills.

issues.title: Zero simplifying-ratio questions despite GL weighting of ~15%
issues.description: GL research estimates simplifying ratios accounts for ~15% of ratio questions. The app has 2 well-designed lessons for this sub-concept (step-by-step and spot-the-mistake) but ZERO questions testing it. The 'simplifying-ratios' sub-concept appears in the orphan list with no questions mapped to it.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 15-20 simplifying ratio questions across D1-D3. D1: simple HCF (divide by 2, 5, 10). D2: larger HCF, partially simplified distractors. D3: three-part ratios, ratios requiring unit conversion before simplifying (e.g. 2km : 500m).

issues.title: Zero ratio-to-fraction conversion questions — GL's #1 exploited misconception
issues.description: GL research explicitly states that part-to-whole confusion (3:4 interpreted as 3/4 instead of 3/7) is 'the single most common error children make with ratios' and is 'heavily exploited' by GL. There are zero questions testing this skill. The orphan sub-concept 'ratio-word-problems' exists with no questions, and there is no sub-concept for ratio-to-fraction at all.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 10-15 ratio-to-fraction questions. E.g. 'The ratio of boys to girls is 3:4. What fraction of the class are boys?' with options including both 3/4 (trap) and 3/7 (correct). Include reverse direction too: 'What ratio?' given a fraction.

issues.title: Difficulty distribution skews D2-heavy (54% vs target 40%)
issues.description: Current distribution: D1:38 (21%), D2:98 (54%), D3:44 (24%). GL target is approximately 30:40:30. D1 is 9 percentage points below target and D2 is 14 points above. This means children practising ratio will face disproportionately medium-difficulty questions without enough easy confidence-builders or enough hard stretch questions.
issues.suggestedFix: When adding new questions (simplifying, ratio-to-fraction), weight them towards D1 and D3. Also consider downgrading some current D2 questions that have clean numbers and single-step solutions (see individual flagged items below).

issues.title: Q16 rated D2 but is straightforward D1 difficulty
issues.description: Ratio 7:3, 21 red balls. Find blue. Scale factor is 3 (21/7=3), blue = 3x3 = 9. This is a single-step problem with small, friendly numbers and a clean scale factor of 3. The question is structurally identical to Q4 (D1) and Q9 (D1). It meets all D1 criteria from GL research: simple two-part ratio, small numbers, single-step, clean division.
issues.suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1.

issues.title: Q20 rated D2 but has D1 characteristics
issues.description: Ratio 9:2, 18 cars. Scale factor = 2, vans = 4. Single-step with a very clean scale factor of 2. The ratio is simple and the numbers are small. Compare with Q4 (D1, scale factor 3) and Q9 (D1, scale factor 5).
issues.suggestedFix: Consider downgrading to D1.

issues.title: Q115 rated D3 but is a clean D1/D2 problem
issues.description: Recipe for 8 cupcakes uses 3 eggs. How many for 24? Scale factor = 24/8 = 3. Eggs = 3x3 = 9. This is a single-step problem with a clean integer scale factor. GL D3 criteria require multi-step problems, three-part ratios, or non-obvious contexts. This question is simpler than many D2 questions in the bank.
issues.suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1 (clean scale factor of 3, simple context).

issues.title: Q1 rated D2 but is a standard D1 sharing problem
issues.description: 30 children, ratio 3:2. How many boys? 5 parts, 30/5=6, boys=3x6=18. This uses small, friendly numbers with clean division. The GL research D1 example is almost identical: 'Share 20 sweets between Tom and Amy in the ratio 3:2'. Q1 uses 30 with 3:2 — barely harder.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider downgrading to D1, or leave as borderline D1/D2 since it does require a sharing calculation.

issues.title: Q177 refers to 'lake length' but asks about riding 'around' it — ambiguous
issues.description: The question says 'A lake is 3.5 cm long on the map' but then asks 'A cyclist rides around the actual lake'. Riding 'around' a lake means the perimeter/circumference, but we are only given the length. If the lake is 3.5cm long on the map (=14km actual), the question treats 14km as the distance to ride around it, but this is the length, not the circumference. A child could reasonably object that you need more information to determine the distance around a lake from just its length.
issues.suggestedFix: Reword to 'A cyclist rides along a road that follows the edge of the lake. The road is 3.5 cm long on the map.' or change to 'A cyclist rides from one end of the lake to the other.'

issues.title: Q6 is more SDT (Speed/Distance/Time) than ratio — cross-topic overlap
issues.description: A car travels 60 miles in 1 hour, how far in 3.5 hours? This is a straightforward speed-distance-time problem (60 x 3.5 = 210). While it uses proportional reasoning, it's essentially from the SDT topic. This pattern repeats for Q21, Q36, Q51, Q69, Q81, Q99, Q117 — all are distance/time/speed problems mapped to the 'unitary-method' sub-concept under ratio. GL would classify these under a different section.
issues.suggestedFix: These questions are fine conceptually (unitary method IS ratio), but consider whether 22 speed/distance/rate problems in a 180-question ratio bank is too many (~12%). The child may feel they're revising SDT not ratio. A few is fine; 22 may dilute ratio-specific practice.

issues.title: Q17 is pure division, not ratio — 'average weight of one chocolate'
issues.description: 240g box with 20 chocolates, find average weight. This is 240/20 = 12g — a simple division problem. While it uses the unitary method concept, it doesn't test ratio understanding at all. Similarly Q47 (150 pencils, 600g, average weight = 4g). These are division problems dressed up as ratio.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep as they provide easy wins, but acknowledge they don't test ratio skills. Could be reclassified or replaced with actual ratio problems at D1.

issues.title: Duplicate option in simplifying-ratios-mistake interact screen
issues.description: The interact screen for 'What does 16:12 simplify to?' has options: ['4:3', '8:6', '8:6', '3:4', '1:1']. The option '8:6' appears twice. This reduces the effective number of distractors from 4 to 3 and looks like a data entry error.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace one '8:6' with a different plausible distractor, e.g. '4:4' (common error of rounding) or '8:3' (dividing only one side incorrectly).

issues.title: Ratio notation lessons exist but have zero questions mapped
issues.description: The 'ratio-notation' sub-concept has 2 well-designed lessons teaching what the colon means and that order matters, but appears in the orphan sub-concepts list with 0 questions. While GL doesn't heavily test notation directly, the lessons would never be triggered via Focused Learning if there's nothing to practise.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 simple D1 questions testing notation understanding. E.g. 'The ratio of cats to dogs is 2:5. Which statement is true?' or 'What does the ratio 3:7 mean?' These would also help boost the D1 count.

issues.title: Orphan sub-concept 'ratio-word-problems' has no lessons and no questions
issues.description: The mappings file lists 'ratio-word-problems' as an orphan sub-concept with 0 questions. It appears to be a placeholder that was never populated.
issues.suggestedFix: Either merge its intended coverage into existing sub-concepts (most word problems are already covered by finding-missing-values, scaling-recipes, etc.) or remove the orphan entry to avoid confusion.

issues.title: Unitary method lesson uses cost context but many mapped questions are speed/distance
issues.description: The unitary method step-by-step lesson teaches via a pencil-cost example ('5 pencils cost £15'), and the visual discovery lesson uses sweet costs. But many questions mapped to this sub-concept are speed/distance/time problems (Q6: car speed, Q21: train speed, Q36: cyclist, Q51: bus, etc.). The teach content says 'Divide the total cost by the number of items to find what one costs' — the word 'cost' doesn't generalise well to speed/distance contexts for a 9-year-old.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add a third lesson variant using a distance/speed context, or update the teach summary text to use more generic language like 'Divide the total by the number to find the value of one unit'.

issues.title: Q97 rated D3 but is a straightforward D2 recipe scaling
issues.description: 30 truffles use 5 eggs, how many for 54? Eggs per truffle = 1/6. 54/6 = 9. While the per-unit value is a fraction (1/6), the scale factor approach is simpler: 54/30 = 1.8, 5 x 1.8 = 9. This is a two-step calculation with manageable numbers. GL D3 requires 3+ calculation steps or combining ratio with other topics.
issues.suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2.

issues.title: Many D2 questions use arithmetic-sequence distractors instead of GL-style error-based distractors
issues.description: Numerous questions use options that are simply evenly spaced numbers around the correct answer (e.g. Q38: 600g, 680g, 700g, 720g, 750g; Q43: 400g, 425g, 450g, 475g, 500g). GL research shows distractors should correspond to specific predictable errors: reversed share, dividing by one part instead of sum, additive error, part-to-whole confusion. The current approach makes it easy to guess (pick the middle option) and doesn't trap common misconceptions.
issues.suggestedFix: For key questions, redesign distractors to include: (1) the reversed share, (2) the additive error answer, (3) an incomplete-calculation answer, (4) a round-number magnet. Not every question needs rework, but the most-used D2 questions should have at least one trap distractor.

issues.title: Q71 total days exceed April — minor realism issue
issues.description: Ratio of sunny:rainy in April is 5:1, with 25 sunny days. This gives 25+5=30 days, which matches April exactly. Not technically wrong, but leaving zero days unaccounted for is unusual. A real April might have overcast days that are neither sunny nor rainy. Minor realism nit.
issues.suggestedFix: No action needed — mathematically correct and the context works. Flagging for completeness only.

issues.title: Three-part ratio questions only appear at D3 — missing D2 coverage
issues.description: GL research notes three-part ratios at ~8% weighting and suggests they appear at D2-D3 difficulty. Currently all three-part ratio questions are D3 (Q146, Q148, Q150, Q152, Q156, Q159, Q162, Q163, Q165, Q166, Q169, Q171, Q172, Q174). A child encountering three-part ratios for the first time would only see them at the hardest difficulty. GL's D2 example explicitly includes: 'Hetta, Ruby and Eva share 250 sweets in the ratio 5:3:2. How many does Ruby get?'
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-8 D2 three-part ratio questions with straightforward totals and clean division. E.g. 'Share 120 in ratio 2:3:5. How much does the middle share get?'

issues.title: Map scale questions have 11 at D1 — high proportion of easy questions for this sub-concept
issues.description: Scale Factors for Maps and Models has 22 questions: 11 D1, 6 D2, 5 D3. Half the questions are D1, which is appropriate for the simple 'multiply map distance by scale' pattern, but means a child doing focused learning on this sub-concept will get a lot of very similar easy questions. The D1 questions (Q12, Q29, Q40, Q59, Q77, Q89, Q107, Q125, Q133, Q137, Q142) all follow the exact same pattern: 1cm = Xkm, distance is Y cm, answer is X*Y km.
issues.suggestedFix: Convert 3-4 of the map D1 questions into simplifying-ratio or ratio-to-fraction D1 questions to reduce repetition and improve coverage of under-served sub-concepts.

issues.title: Scale maps key-fact lesson teaches reverse direction but no questions test it
issues.description: The scale-maps-keyfact lesson teaches going from real life BACK to map distance (dividing by the scale factor). However, all 22 scale/map questions go in the forward direction only (map to real). No question asks 'The real distance is 40km, the scale is 1cm:5km, how far on the map?' This means the lesson teaches a skill that is never practised.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 3-5 questions testing reverse scale conversion (real to map). These naturally fit at D2-D3 difficulty since children find division harder than multiplication.

issues.title: Correct answer heavily concentrated at position index 2 (C)
issues.description: A large proportion of questions have correct answer at index 2 (the middle option, C). While I haven't counted every instance, a quick scan shows a clear clustering. GL explicitly distributes answers roughly evenly across A-E. A pattern-aware child could exploit this by defaulting to C when unsure.
issues.suggestedFix: Audit answer position distribution and redistribute so each position (0-4) gets roughly 20% of correct answers. This is a lower priority cosmetic fix.
